svg.text: Two argument patterns sound similar, but one is reliable and the other is a classic trap.
Start with the concrete case: Two argument patterns sound similar, but one is reliable and the other is a classic trap.
Name the hidden structure: Pattern cards for common valid and invalid forms in everyday language.
Watch for the trap: Relying on surface similarity rather than actual structure.
Transfer the habit immediately: Sort six arguments into valid and invalid forms and explain why.

Unlike broad intro courses, this lesson turns common valid and invalid forms into a practical operating skill through a real scenario, a visual model, a retrieval gate, and a transfer task built around argument mapping, evidence discipline, and fair-minded judgment.
Relying on surface similarity rather than actual structure.

Sort six arguments into valid and invalid forms and explain why.
The goal is not to memorize the label, but to practice a repeatable move in real judgment. Ask the learner to produce a concrete artifact, not just an opinion.
